exercises.title: Read a Gatling report summary
exercises.instructions:
```
Gatling simulation report:
  Total requests:    14,940
  Successful:        14,907  (99.78%)
  Failed:               33   (0.22%)
  Mean response time:   51ms
  95th percentile:     142ms
  99th percentile:     231ms
  Max response time:  1,847ms

Assertions:
  p99 < 200ms → FAILED (231ms)
  error rate < 0.1% → FAILED (0.22%)
  throughput > 45 req/s → OK
```
1. How many assertions passed and failed?
2. What does the failed p99 assertion mean in production terms?
3. What would you investigate first?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. 1 assertion PASSED (throughput), 2 assertions FAILED (p99, error rate)

2. p99 = 231ms > SLO of 200ms means:
   1% of requests (149 out of 14,940) took > 200ms.
   In production at 10,000 req/min: 100 requests/min are too slow.
   Users experiencing > 200ms latency: 1 in 100 — affects UX and payment conversions.

3. Investigate first: the 33 failed requests.
   Failed requests → logs → look for: connection refused, 5xx errors, timeouts.
   Likely causes: pool exhaustion (if spiky load), a slow query (if consistent), or
   resource limit (if at peak of load test).
   Then: p99 = 231ms (only 31ms over SLO) — look at what the 99th-percentile request was doing:
   distributed traces (Zipkin) during load test → find the slow path.

test_cases.expected:
1. 1 assertion PASSED (throughput), 2 assertions FAILED (p99, error rate)

2. p99 = 231ms > SLO of 200ms means:
   1% of requests (149 out of 14,940) took > 200ms.
   In production at 10,000 req/min: 100 requests/min are too slow.
   Users experiencing > 200ms latency: 1 in 100 — affects UX and payment conversions.

3. Investigate first: the 33 failed requests.
   Failed requests → logs → look for: connection refused, 5xx errors, timeouts.
   Likely causes: pool exhaustion (if spiky load), a slow query (if consistent), or
   resource limit (if at peak of load test).
   Then: p99 = 231ms (only 31ms over SLO) — look at what the 99th-percentile request was doing:
   distributed traces (Zipkin) during load test → find the slow path.

hints: p99 failure: 1% of requests exceed the threshold
33 failed requests: check error type in Gatling report errors tab
31ms over SLO: likely a fixable bottleneck (not fundamentally broken)

exercises.title: Complete the Gatling load profile
exercises.instructions: Fill in a 3-phase load profile: warmup → ramp → sustain.
hints: atOnceUsers: immediate users for JIT warmup
rampUsers: gradual traffic increase (realistic)
constantUsersPerSec: sustained load for measurement

exercises.title: Write a basic Gatling simulation
exercises.instructions:
Write a minimal Gatling simulation for Razorpay's GET /api/health endpoint:
1. HttpProtocol pointing to staging URL
2. Scenario: GET /api/health, check status 200
3. Load: 10 users immediately, ramp to 50 in 30s
4. Assertions: p99 < 100ms, success > 99.9%
hints: Simulation class: extends Simulation, static initializer {}  holds setUp()
.shareConnections(): reuse TCP connections (realistic — browsers do this)
.pause(): think time between requests (models real user behavior)

exercises.title: Gatling feeder for test data variety
hints: .random(): each virtual user picks randomly from CSV (realistic load distribution)
#{variable}: Gatling session expression — inject feeder values into requests
1000 rows: ensures cache is exercised realistically (mix of hits and misses)

exercises.title: Gatling correlation: capture and reuse response data
exercises.instructions:
Payment flow: POST /payments → capture paymentId → GET /payments/{id} to check status.
1. .check(jsonPath('$.id').saveAs('paymentId'))
2. Second request: GET /payments/#{paymentId}
3. Check second response status is 200
4. Add assertions: p99 < 300ms for the full flow
hints: saveAs: stores response field in virtual user session
#{paymentId}: retrieves saved value from session
jsonPath.in(): accepts multiple valid values

exercises.title: Spike test and soak test profiles
exercises.instructions:
Write two different load profiles:
1. Spike test: normal → sudden 10× spike → back to normal
2. Soak test: moderate load sustained for 30 minutes
3. Explain: what each test reveals

expected_stdout_contains: Spike: tests resilience. Soak: detects leaks over time.

hints: Spike: rampUsersPerSec(50).to(500).during(10) → 10× spike in 10 seconds
Soak: 30 minutes at constant load → memory/connection leaks become visible
constantUsersPerSec: arrival rate model (realistic for open systems like HTTP APIs)

exercises.title: Gatling Maven plugin CI integration
exercises.instructions:
Configure Gatling to run in GitHub Actions:
1. pom.xml: gatling-maven-plugin 4.6.0
2. GitHub Actions job: run Gatling test on staging
3. Upload Gatling report as artifact
4. Fail the job if assertions fail
5. Explain: when to run load tests in CI vs on demand
exercises.expected_solution:
<!-- pom.xml -->
<plugin>
  <groupId>io.gatling</groupId>
  <artifactId>gatling-maven-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>4.6.0</version>
  <configuration>
    <simulationClass>com.razorpay.simulation.PaymentLoadSimulation</simulationClass>
    <resultsFolder>target/gatling/results</resultsFolder>
    <!-- failOnError: true by default — Gatling fails build if assertions fail -->
  </configuration>
</plugin>

# GitHub Actions job:
# .github/workflows/load-test.yml:
# jobs:
#   load-test:
#     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
#     environment: staging       # manual approval gate (don't auto-run on every PR)
#     steps:
#     - uses: actions/checkout@v4
#     - uses: actions/setup-java@v4
#       with: { java-version: '21', distribution: temurin, cache: maven }
#     - name: Run Gatling load test
#       run: mvn gatling:test -DsimulationClass=PaymentLoadSimulation
#       env:
#         GATLING_BASE_URL: https://staging.razorpay.internal
#     - name: Upload Gatling report
#       if: always()          # upload even if test fails
#       uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
#       with:
#         name: gatling-report
#         path: target/gatling/results/

# WHEN TO RUN:
# On demand: before major releases (not on every commit — too slow for CI)
# Weekly scheduled: regression detection
# After infrastructure changes: verify new k8s node type handles load
# Pre-peak: before Diwali sale, IPO day, budget announcement

# NOT suitable for: every PR (5+ minutes per run, generates staging load)

public class GatlingCIGuide { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Gatling CI: environment gate + upload report + on-demand trigger");
} }
hints: environment: staging: prevents load tests on prod and requires approval
if: always() on upload: get report even when test fails (you need it most then!)
mvn gatling:test exits non-zero if any assertion fails → fails the CI job

exercises.title: Load test Zerodha order service: full simulation
exercises.instructions:
Write complete Gatling simulation for Zerodha order service:
1. Feeder: CSV with symbols, quantities, order types
2. Scenario: POST /orders (place order) → check orderId
3. Load: 10 warmup → ramp 200 in 30s → sustain 100/s for 2min
4. Assertions: p99 < 100ms (trading must be fast), error < 0.01%
5. Spike: additional spike test profile
hints: Trading SLO: 100ms p99 (stricter than payment 200ms)
0.01% error rate: one lost trade per 10,000 → financial damage
Spike after sustained: market open surge after warming up

exercises.title: Prometheus metrics during load test
exercises.instructions:
Correlate Gatling load test with Prometheus metrics:
1. Run Gatling at 500 req/s for 5 minutes
2. During test: what Prometheus metrics to watch
3. Record: baseline and loaded values
4. Write a @SpringBootTest that validates key metrics are registered
hints: Watch gc.pause during load test: rising pauses = memory pressure under load
HikariCP pending > 0 during load = pool exhaustion (fix: increase pool or optimize queries)
Cache hit rate should stay > 80% during load (feeder provides variety, but popular items still hit cache)

exercises.title: What does this load test reveal?
exercises.instructions:
Load test results (500 users, 5 minutes):
```
Time    | req/s | p99    | Heap  | GC pause | HikariPending
0:00    | 0     | —      | 512MB | 0ms      | 0
0:30    | 50    | 45ms   | 580MB | 12ms     | 0
1:00    | 100   | 48ms   | 650MB | 15ms     | 0
2:00    | 200   | 95ms   | 820MB | 25ms     | 0
3:00    | 350   | 143ms  | 1.1GB | 78ms     | 3
4:00    | 500   | 312ms  | 1.4GB | 189ms    | 12
5:00    | 500   | 487ms  | 1.7GB | 312ms    | 25
```

1. What is the primary bottleneck?
2. At what load did things go wrong?
3. What is the root cause?
4. How would you fix it?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. PRIMARY BOTTLENECK: JVM heap + GC pause cascade.
   p99 rises sharply as heap grows. GC pause at 5:00 = 312ms
   → every GC stop-the-world adds 312ms to any in-flight request.

2. THINGS GO WRONG at ~350 req/s (3:00):
   p99 goes from 95ms (OK) to 143ms (borderline) at 350 req/s.
   At 500 req/s: p99=487ms (2.4× over 200ms SLO). HikariPending=25 → pool exhausted.

3. ROOT CAUSE:
   Memory leak OR too-small heap for this traffic level.
   Evidence: Heap grows from 512MB to 1.7GB over 5 minutes at constant traffic.
   Normal: heap should plateau (GC reclaims objects). Growing heap = objects not collected.
   Likely: cache growing unboundedly, or ThreadLocal not cleared,
   or large objects created per request not freed.
   Secondary: pool exhaustion (pending=25) caused by GC pauses (threads blocked
   during GC can't return connections to pool).

4. FIX:
   a) Take heap dump during load test: jmap -dump at 4:00 → analyze with MAT
   b) Check for: unbounded Caffeine (no maximumSize), ThreadLocal not cleared
   c) Fix root cause (likely: add maximumSize to Caffeine cache)
   d) Re-run load test: heap should plateau, p99 should stay flat
   e) If still problematic: increase JVM MaxRAMPercentage or add another pod

test_cases.expected:
1. PRIMARY BOTTLENECK: JVM heap + GC pause cascade.
   p99 rises sharply as heap grows. GC pause at 5:00 = 312ms
   → every GC stop-the-world adds 312ms to any in-flight request.

2. THINGS GO WRONG at ~350 req/s (3:00):
   p99 goes from 95ms (OK) to 143ms (borderline) at 350 req/s.
   At 500 req/s: p99=487ms (2.4× over 200ms SLO). HikariPending=25 → pool exhausted.

3. ROOT CAUSE:
   Memory leak OR too-small heap for this traffic level.
   Evidence: Heap grows from 512MB to 1.7GB over 5 minutes at constant traffic.
   Normal: heap should plateau (GC reclaims objects). Growing heap = objects not collected.
   Likely: cache growing unboundedly, or ThreadLocal not cleared,
   or large objects created per request not freed.
   Secondary: pool exhaustion (pending=25) caused by GC pauses (threads blocked
   during GC can't return connections to pool).

4. FIX:
   a) Take heap dump during load test: jmap -dump at 4:00 → analyze with MAT
   b) Check for: unbounded Caffeine (no maximumSize), ThreadLocal not cleared
   c) Fix root cause (likely: add maximumSize to Caffeine cache)
   d) Re-run load test: heap should plateau, p99 should stay flat
   e) If still problematic: increase JVM MaxRAMPercentage or add another pod

hints: Rising heap over time = objects not GC'd = memory leak or unbounded cache
GC pause → p99 spike: 312ms pause adds to every request in-flight
HikariPending spike follows GC: threads stuck in GC can't return DB connections

exercises.title: Gatling with authentication flow
exercises.instructions:
Payment API requires JWT. Simulate:
1. First request: POST /auth/token → save JWT from response
2. Subsequent requests: use JWT in Authorization header
3. Token refresh after 50 requests (simulating expiry)
4. Assertions: auth flow p99 < 50ms, payment p99 < 200ms
hints: details("requestName"): assertion on specific request (not global)
repeat(N).on(chain): repeat the chain N times per virtual user
Token refresh: real-world pattern — don't use same token forever in load test

exercises.title: M22 end-to-end performance validation
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest that validates all M22 optimizations are in place:
1. JVM heap gauge registered
2. HikariCP pool max > 0
3. Cache hit rate gauge registered
4. HTTP request timer registered (http.server.requests)
5. All 4 assertions pass in one test class
hints: jvm.memory.used: always present with spring-boot-starter-actuator
hikaricp.connections.max: registered when HikariCP + MeterRegistry both present
meter count > 15: Spring Boot registers JVM, GC, HikariCP, executor, etc. automatically

exercises.title: Capacity planning from load test results
exercises.instructions:
From load test: payment service handles 200 req/s with p99=150ms (SLO=200ms) at 50% CPU.

1. Calculate: what is the theoretical max req/s before SLO violation?
2. Calculate: how many pods needed to handle Diwali sale (1500 req/s)?
3. Write a @SpringBootTest with capacity calculation logic
4. Show: buffer recommendation (80% capacity rule)
hints: safeCapacity = (req/s / cpuPercent) × maxCPU × 0.80: 80% rule
ceil(1500/320) = 5 pods minimum
40% buffer: 1 pod for rolling update + 1 for failure tolerance + autoscaler lag

exercises.title: Load test results in CI: fail the build
exercises.instructions:
Integrate Gatling into GitHub Actions pipeline:
1. Run after deploy-staging succeeds
2. Fail the job if p99 > 200ms OR error rate > 0.1%
3. Upload report as artifact
4. On failure: trigger rollback job
5. Show: complete GitHub Actions workflow snippet
exercises.expected_solution:
# .github/workflows/performance-gate.yml
name: Performance Gate

on:
  workflow_call:     # called by main CI/CD pipeline after deploy-staging
    inputs:
      image_tag:
        type: string
        required: true

jobs:
  load-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging  # requires staging env to be running
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-java@v4
      with: { java-version: '21', distribution: temurin, cache: maven }
    
    - name: Run Gatling performance test
      run: |
        mvn gatling:test \
          -DsimulationClass=com.razorpay.PaymentLoadSimulation \
          -DbaseUrl=${{ vars.STAGING_URL }}
      # mvn exits non-zero if ANY assertion fails
      # → GitHub Actions marks step as FAILED
      # → job FAILED → downstream jobs (deploy-prod) BLOCKED
    
    - name: Upload Gatling report
      if: always()  # upload even on failure
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
      with:
        name: gatling-report-${{ inputs.image_tag }}
        path: target/gatling/results/
        retention-days: 30
    
    - name: Rollback staging if load test failed
      if: failure()
      run: |
        echo '${{ secrets.KUBECONFIG_STAGING }}' > /tmp/kube.conf
        kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service -n payments
        kubectl rollout status deployment/payment-service -n payments --timeout=3m
      env:
        KUBECONFIG: /tmp/kube.conf

# Main pipeline caller:
# deploy-prod:
#   needs: [deploy-staging, load-test]  ← blocked if load-test fails!
#   ...

public class PerformanceGateDoc { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("Performance gate: load test blocks prod deploy");
} }

expected_stdout_contains: Performance gate: load test blocks prod deploy

hints: workflow_call: reusable — called from main CI/CD pipeline
mvn gatling:test exits non-zero on assertion failure → job fails → prod deploy blocked
rollback if: failure() → automatic rollback on performance regression

exercises.title: Complete M22 synthesis — all optimizations validated
exercises.instructions:
Build the final Module 22 synthesis:
1. Capacity planner test (safeCapacity from observed metrics)
2. All M22 metrics registered and validated (JVM + HikariCP + Cache)
3. Minimal Gatling simulation class (compiles) with SLO assertions
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): metrics registered, capacity math correct, simulation structure valid

All 3 tests pass.
hints: assertNotNull(reg.find(...).gauge()): verifies auto-registered metrics
M22CapacityMath: pure math — no Spring context needed
assertDoesNotThrow(() -> new M22SynthesisSimulation()): Gatling simulation instantiation test
